primary_diagnosis.name: Chronic limb-threatening ischaemia (critical limb ischaemia) with an arterial (ischaemic) ankle ulcer and dry gangrene, acutely exacerbated

pathophysiology_summary: Long-standing peripheral atherosclerosis reduces arterial inflow to the distal limb below the level needed to perfuse resting tissue. This produces ischaemic rest pain (characteristically worse when supine or the leg is elevated, because gravity assistance to perfusion is lost, and relieved by dependency), non-healing 'punched-out' arterial ulceration typically over pressure/bony points such as the lateral malleolus, and — as perfusion fails further — tissue necrosis with dry black eschar (dry gangrene). Critical/chronic limb-threatening ischaemia is defined by ischaemic rest pain (>2 weeks) and/or tissue loss with objectively reduced perfusion, and carries a high risk of major amputation without urgent revascularisation. The absent distal pulses, cold pale hairless shiny skin and prolonged capillary refill reflect severe hypoperfusion. Crucially, the standard management levers for the common venous ulcer — compression bandaging and leg elevation — are harmful here: they further reduce arterial inflow and worsen ischaemia.

differential.evidence_basis: Non-healing ankle ulcer with severe rest pain worse on elevation and relieved by dependency in an elderly patient — the hallmark of arterial insufficiency.
A common cause of leg ulcers, but venous ulcers are typically less painful, sit over the gaiter/medial malleolus, and improve with elevation — the opposite of this pattern.
Considered in an ulcerated foot, but neuropathic ulcers are usually painless and the foot is warm — inconsistent with exquisite pain and a cold foot.
Must be screened for because superimposed infection changes urgency — but there is no fever, spreading erythema, warmth or purulence.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI should widen to the ulcer differentials and specifically ask the questions that separate arterial from venous (effect of elevation, foot temperature, pain severity).

differential.diagnosis: critical limb ischaemia with arterial ulcer
differential.evidence_basis: Rest pain relieved by dependency + cold pale foot + non-healing punched-out ulcer with dry black eschar = limb-threatening ischaemia.
Pain worsens with elevation, foot is cold, skin is pale/shiny with hair loss, no haemosiderin staining or dependent oedema — not venous.
No fever, no spreading erythema, no warmth, no purulence — infection not the driver, though it remains a safety-net trigger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the elevation/dependency pattern and cold foot are elicited, arterial ischaemia is the clear leader and compression/elevation must be actively avoided.

differential.diagnosis: acutely exacerbated critical limb ischaemia with arterial ulcer and dry gangrene
differential.evidence_basis: Limb-threatening ischaemia requiring urgent in-person vascular assessment; telehealth cannot confirm perfusion or safely manage the pain crisis.
The 5-day rapid deterioration raises the possibility of progression to acute occlusion (the 6 Ps) — a 000 emergency if the foot becomes suddenly mottled, numb or paralysed.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Endpoint is recognition + urgent in-person escalation, with explicit safety-netting for acute limb ischaemia and for infection.

red_flags.description: Ischaemic rest pain — severe nocturnal foot pain worse on elevation and relieved only by dependency
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rest pain indicates perfusion inadequate even at rest — a defining feature of critical/chronic limb-threatening ischaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to irreversible tissue loss and major amputation; the pain crisis itself drives exhaustion and falls risk.

red_flags.description: Cold, pale, shiny, hairless limb with clinically absent distal perfusion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signs of severe arterial hypoperfusion; the limb is at imminent risk without revascularisation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed critical ischaemia; delay to vascular assessment costs the limb.

red_flags.description: Tissue loss with dry black eschar (dry gangrene) over the lateral malleolus
red_flags.clinical_significance: Established necrosis places this in the limb-threatening category (tissue loss) of chronic limb-threatening ischaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage as a routine chronic wound; loss of the revascularisation window.

red_flags.description: Rapid deterioration over 5 days with intractable pain and 72 hours of sleep deprivation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rapid change signals possible progression toward acute-on-chronic limb ischaemia; the exhaustion adds a high falls risk in a person living alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed acute occlusion; collapse/fall at home with no one present.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a weeks-old leg ulcer as routine wound care and arranging a non-urgent dressing/review, missing that this is limb-threatening ischaemia.
Assuming any leg ulcer is venous and advising compression bandaging or stockings — in arterial disease compression further chokes inflow and can precipitate gangrene.
Giving the standard 'elevate the leg' oedema/wound advice; elevation worsens ischaemic rest pain and perfusion. The leg should be kept dependent.
Interpreting the black eschar as infection and reaching for empiric antibiotics, when there are no infective features and the driver is ischaemia.
Attempting to manage the pain and wound remotely rather than escalating; pulses/ABI cannot be assessed by chat and opioids cannot be initiated remotely.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Stage IV (ischaemic ulceration/rest pain with tissue loss)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Critical limb ischaemia — urgent vascular assessment for revascularisation.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Category 5 (rest pain with minor tissue loss)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Chronic limb-threatening ischaemia; limb at risk without timely intervention.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses
Ankle-brachial pressure index / handheld Doppler signals
Exact blood pressure and heart rate
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a limb-threatening circulation problem and escalate to urgent same-day in-person assessment (emergency department or urgent vascular). Advise NOT to apply compression and NOT to elevate the leg; keep it dependent and warm and the wound clean and dry. Safety-net explicitly for acute limb ischaemia and for infection (000/ED).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for recognising ischaemia (the pattern is classic); the real risk is UNDER-action — routine wound care or a non-urgent referral — and the compression/elevation error.
